interpretation: The Imperial Rescripts institutionalized a maximally strong symbolic order in which the kokutai (national polity) organized identity, legitimacy, obligation, memory, and sacrifice around the Emperor as a divine collective body. Individual survival was explicitly subordinated to imperial survival, and death in service of the Emperor was framed as the highest moral fulfillment.

confidence.label: moderate
confidence.rationale: Structural alignment with the theory is strong; chain is a minimal stub requiring expansion with wartime sources and secondary scholarship before scholarly use.

mechanismSummary: The imperial symbolic order represented the national body as divinely eternal; individual death in service of that body was encoded as participation in its immortality, making sacrifice the highest moral act.

interpretation: The draft evidence chain supports a preliminary reading of the Nazi Volksgemeinschaft as an imagined collective body whose sacred survival justified individual sacrifice.

interpretation: The Basic Law's inviolable human dignity provision (Art. 1 GG) and eternity clause (Art. 79(3) GG) are direct constitutional embodiments of high corrigibility: the postwar symbolic order structurally limits its own capacity for pathological escalation by placing individual dignity beyond collective override.

confidence.label: moderate-high
confidence.rationale: Interpretation is well-grounded in the constitutional text and contrasts cleanly with the Nazi Germany sacrificial-escalation case. Chain is currently a minimal stub.

mechanismSummary: The founding constitutional document embeds human dignity as inviolable and unamendable, directly blocking the collective-body logic that characterized the preceding pathological order.

interpretation: The Soviet symbolic order had historically high strength (Marxist-Leninist ideology organizing identity, legitimacy, obligation, and sacrifice across a vast institutional apparatus), but by 1985-1991 that strength had eroded to the point where the order could not survive sustained crisis pressure. Gorbachev's admission of systemic failure and the formal dissolution of the USSR mark the terminal phase of legitimacy collapse: the symbolic order could neither sustain its founding claims nor suppress the centrifugal forces its rigidity had generated.

confidence.label: moderate
confidence.rationale: Legitimacy-collapse reading is well-supported by the dissolution evidence; the precise trajectory from strength to collapse requires fuller periodization and secondary source corroboration.

mechanismSummary: The Soviet symbolic order's inability to acknowledge systemic failure until too late — a decades-long suppression of correction — depleted the legitimacy of its founding claims, and the reform process that might have saved the order instead revealed and accelerated its disintegration.
